content.text: Efficiently directing work to the right model, endpoint, or agent tier is one of the most consequential engineering choices in a production agentic system. Request routing is the discipline of making that decision automatically and continuously, balancing latency, cost, quality, and capacity across a fleet of heterogeneous inference backends.
What is Request Routing?

Request routing in agentic AI systems is the process of inspecting each incoming task and dispatching it to the most appropriate model or compute path before any generation begins. Rather than sending every request to a single monolithic model, a router classifies the request along several dimensions — complexity, domain, latency requirement, cost budget, and safety tier — and selects the backend best suited to handle it given current system state.
A router is distinct from a load balancer in a traditional web stack. A web load balancer distributes identical requests across equivalent servers; an AI request router distributes
qualitatively different
tasks across
qualitatively different

backends. A trivial calendar lookup and a multi-step legal reasoning task both arrive as natural-language strings, but the optimal model for each may differ by two orders of magnitude in cost and three times in latency budget. Routing closes that gap.

The router itself can be as simple as a rule-based classifier that looks at token count or keyword tags, or as sophisticated as a learned meta-model that scores each candidate backend on expected answer quality for a given prompt. In practice, most production systems start with heuristics and evolve toward learned classifiers as signal accumulates.
Why it matters

At scale, the economic pressure is clear: if every request is handled by the most capable (and most expensive) model available, inference costs grow linearly with request volume even when 70–80% of requests do not need that capability. Surveys of production AI workloads consistently show that the majority of requests fall into a low-complexity category that a smaller, faster model can handle at equivalent quality for the user.

Beyond cost, routing determines user-perceived latency. A system that sends a simple FAQ lookup to the same 70B-parameter model as a complex code refactoring task will impose unnecessary wait times on the easy case. Segregating by complexity makes the fast path genuinely fast — often sub-200 ms — while reserving capacity on the slow, expensive path for tasks that genuinely require it.

Agentic pipelines add another dimension: tool-use chains and multi-hop reasoning amplify any single-request inefficiency. A poorly routed first step can cause an agent to retry, escalate, or fail, cascading the cost and latency penalty across the entire chain. Correct routing at the entry point prevents these downstream amplifications.
Benefits
Cost reduction:
Routing 60–80% of requests to a smaller model can cut inference spend by 50–80% while maintaining overall quality because those requests do not need the larger model's capabilities.
Lower tail latency:
Fast-path requests avoid the queue and generation time of heavy models, driving p95 and p99 latencies down significantly for the majority of users.
Higher throughput:
By offloading easy requests, heavy models are freed for the tasks that need them, increasing their effective utilisation and overall system throughput without adding capacity.
Domain specialisation:

Fine-tuned or instruction-tuned specialist models can outperform general models on narrow domains (code generation, medical summarisation, legal analysis). Routing delivers the right specialist rather than compromising with one model.
Resilience and failover:
When one endpoint is degraded, the router can redirect traffic to an alternative — a different provider, a cached response, or a graceful degradation path — without exposing the failure to callers.
Budget enforcement:
Per-user or per-organisation token quotas can be enforced at the router, escalating to a cheaper model or returning a quota-exceeded response before a single token is wasted.
Trade-offs & limitations
The primary risk of routing is
misclassification

. Sending a complex request to the fast path produces a low-quality answer. Sending a trivial request to the expensive path wastes money but at least produces a correct answer — so classification errors are asymmetric: under-routing is costly; over-routing is expensive but safe. The classifier must therefore be calibrated conservatively, erring toward the heavier model when uncertain.

Routing adds latency overhead. Every request must pass through a classification step before dispatch. If the classifier is a model call itself, that overhead can be 50–200 ms, which erodes the gains on fast-path requests. Rule-based classifiers and embedding-similarity scorers avoid this penalty; learned neural classifiers impose it.

Maintaining multiple model endpoints increases operational complexity. Each model has its own versioning cadence, API shape, rate limits, and failure modes. The router must normalise these differences, adding a maintenance burden that a single-endpoint system avoids.

Routing decisions are stateless by default but agentic tasks are often stateful. A multi-turn conversation must either route all turns to the same backend (limiting flexibility) or maintain a session affinity map (adding complexity). If the router escalates mid-session from a small to a large model, conversation history must be passed along — potentially exceeding the smaller model's context window in a way that was not apparent at the start.
How it works
The routing decision reduces to a scoring function: given a request
and a set of candidate backends
, select the backend
that maximises expected utility subject to constraints:

attrs.latex: b^* = \arg\max_{b \in B} \; U(q(r, b),\, c(r, b)) \quad \text{s.t.} \; \ell(r, b) \leq L_{\max}

content.text: Where
is predicted answer quality,
is predicted cost,
is predicted latency, and
is the SLA latency ceiling. The utility function
encodes the trade-off the product makes between quality and cost.
In practice, the scoring function is implemented as a cascade of classifiers rather than a single optimiser. The pipeline typically works as follows:
Cache check:
Compute a semantic hash (embedding similarity) of the incoming request. If an exact or near-exact match exists in the response cache, return immediately without any model call.
Complexity estimation:

Score the request on estimated token length, tool-use requirements, and reasoning depth. This can be a simple heuristic (character count + keyword presence for tool calls) or a lightweight embedding-based regression model.
Domain classification:
Tag the request with a domain label (code, math, general, safety-sensitive) using a small classifier. Domain labels determine which specialist pool is eligible.
SLA class assignment:
Map the caller context (synchronous API call, background batch job, streaming interactive session) to a latency budget class.
Backend selection:
Given complexity score, domain tag, SLA class, and current endpoint health metrics, select the cheapest qualifying backend. If the preferred backend is at capacity, escalate to the next tier.
Fallback and retry:
If the selected backend fails or times out, the router retries on the next tier, up to a configured maximum escalation depth. Quality-validated responses can be cached to prevent future misses.

The quality validator deserves special attention in agentic systems: after generation, a lightweight model or rule-based checker can assess whether the response is complete and well-formed. Responses that fail validation are automatically escalated to the next tier for regeneration rather than returned to the caller. This closes the misclassification loop without requiring human intervention.
Architecture overview

The diagram below shows the key components: an incoming request stream, the central router with its classifier and priority queue, four route tiers, a response aggregator that handles validation and caching, and a cross-cutting observability layer that feeds latency and quality signal back into routing decisions.

attrs.alt: Request Routing Architecture — Agentic AI showing four routing tiers, response aggregator, and observability layer

content.text: When to use it
Request routing pays off when the workload has high variance in complexity and the system handles meaningful traffic volume. A few concrete triggers:
Mixed-complexity workloads:
The system handles both simple factual lookups and complex multi-step tasks. If all requests are uniformly complex, there is no easy path to route to.
Cost pressure at scale:
Inference costs are a material line item and the team has data showing that a significant fraction of requests can be satisfied by a cheaper model.
Strict SLA tiers:
The product has interactive sessions that must respond in under 500 ms alongside batch jobs that can tolerate minutes. A single model cannot optimally serve both.
Domain specialisation opportunity:
There is a fine-tuned or instruction-tuned model that demonstrably outperforms the general model on a specific sub-domain present in the workload.
Multi-provider resilience:
The architecture uses more than one inference provider and needs automatic failover when one degrades.
When not to use routing:

If the system serves a uniform task type at low volume, the added complexity outweighs the savings. A single well-chosen model with prompt caching is simpler and sufficient. Routing also adds risk when the classification decision cannot be validated — if there is no way to evaluate whether routing decisions are correct, the system can silently degrade quality on misclassified requests.
How to implement
Instrument existing traffic.

Before building the router, log every request with its full prompt and the human-evaluated or automated-quality-scored response. This dataset is the ground truth the classifier will learn from and the benchmark the routing system will be evaluated against.
Define routing tiers.

Choose two to four backends with meaningfully different cost/quality/latency profiles. A practical starting point: (a) fast small model for cache hits and trivial tasks, (b) mid-tier model for the majority of requests, (c) specialist or full-size model for complex tasks. More tiers increase savings but also increase misclassification surface.
Build the complexity scorer.

Start with a heuristic: estimate token length from character count, count tool-call keywords, flag multi-hop trigger phrases. Validate the heuristic against the ground truth dataset by checking whether requests classified as simple actually received high-quality responses from the small model. Iterate until precision on the simple class exceeds 90%.
Add domain classification.
Train or fine-tune a lightweight classifier (embedding + linear head, or a small distilled model) on the domain labels extracted from the ground truth dataset. Validate on a held-out split.
Implement the dispatch layer.

Write a router class that takes the complexity score and domain label, consults current endpoint health metrics (latency p95, error rate, queue depth), and selects the backend. Normalise the response format across backends so callers see a unified API.
Wire in response validation.

After generation, run a lightweight checker (regex completeness check, JSON schema validation, or a small scoring model) on the response. Failed responses trigger automatic escalation to the next tier and re-generation.
Deploy with shadow mode.

Run the router in shadow mode first: it classifies and logs routing decisions but continues to use the full model for actual responses. Compare the shadow classification against ground truth before cutting over live traffic.
Monitor and retrain.

Track per-tier quality metrics, cost per request, and escalation rate continuously. When the escalation rate rises above a threshold (e.g. 15%), retrain the classifier on recent data. When quality drops on a tier, investigate whether the model was updated or the request distribution shifted.
Examples
Example 1: Rule-based router with tiered dispatch

A rule-based router is the fastest path to production. It uses simple features extracted from the prompt text — token estimate, presence of tool-call markers, domain keywords — without any model inference in the classification step.
Example 2: Embedding-based semantic router with fallback escalation

An embedding-based router uses vector similarity to a set of labelled exemplars to classify requests. This handles paraphrased and novel phrasing better than keyword rules, at the cost of requiring a fast embedding model call.

from __future__ import annotations
import asyncio
import numpy as np
import openai

client = openai.AsyncOpenAI()

# Exemplar prompts per tier (drawn from the ground-truth dataset)
EXEMPLARS: dict[str, list[str]] = {
    "fast": [
        "What year was the Eiffel Tower built?",
        "Convert 100 Fahrenheit to Celsius.",
    ],
    "standard": [
        "Summarise the key differences between REST and GraphQL APIs.",
        "Explain eventual consistency in distributed systems.",
    ],
    "specialist": [
        "Refactor this Python class to use async/await throughout.",
        "Derive the gradient of cross-entropy loss with respect to the logits.",
    ],
}

_exemplar_embeddings: dict[str, np.ndarray] | None = None


async def _embed(texts: list[str]) -> np.ndarray:
    response = await client.embeddings.create(model="text-embedding-3-small", input=texts)
    return np.array([r.embedding for r in response.data])


async def load_exemplar_embeddings() -> None:
    global _exemplar_embeddings
    _exemplar_embeddings = {tier: await _embed(texts) for tier, texts in EXEMPLARS.items()}


def cosine_similarity(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b) + 1e-9))


async def route_semantic(prompt: str) -> str:
    assert _exemplar_embeddings is not None
    prompt_emb = (await _embed([prompt]))[0]
    tier_scores = {
        tier: max(cosine_similarity(prompt_emb, ex) for ex in embs)
        for tier, embs in _exemplar_embeddings.items()
    }
    chosen = max(tier_scores, key=tier_scores.__getitem__)
    # Fall back to standard when confidence is low
    return chosen if tier_scores[chosen] >= 0.75 else "standard"


TIER_MODELS = {"fast": "gpt-4o-mini", "standard": "gpt-4o-mini", "specialist": "gpt-4o"}


async def call_with_routing(prompt: str) -> str:
    tier = await route_semantic(prompt)
    escalation = [tier, "standard", "specialist"]
    seen: set[str] = set()
    tiers_to_try = [t for t in escalation if not (t in seen or seen.add(t))]

    for attempt_tier in tiers_to_try:
        model = TIER_MODELS[attempt_tier]
        response = await client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
            max_tokens=1024,
        )
        content = response.choices[0].message.content or ""
        if len(content.strip()) >= 20:
            print(f"[routing] tier={attempt_tier} model={model}")
            return content
        print(f"[routing] escalating from {attempt_tier}")
    return content


async def main() -> None:
    await load_exemplar_embeddings()
    result = await call_with_routing(
        "Implement a binary search tree with insert and in-order traversal in Python."
    )
    print(result)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())
Real-world example

Consider a customer support automation platform processing 500,000 requests per day across 300 enterprise tenants. Without routing, every request goes to a high-capability model at a cost of approximately $0.015 per request, totalling $7,500/day. Analysis of a 30-day traffic sample reveals:
42% of requests are simple FAQ lookups ("What is your refund policy?", "What are your support hours?") with median prompt length under 120 tokens.
38% are account-specific questions that require retrieving data via tool calls but are otherwise straightforward.
14% are complex multi-turn escalation handling that requires reasoning over conversation history and policy documents.
6% are sensitive cases (billing disputes, legal queries) that must use the most capable model and a separate safety-reviewed system prompt.

The engineering team implements a three-tier router: a fast-path small model for FAQ lookups (42%), a mid-tier model for tool-use requests (38%), and the full model retained for complex and sensitive cases (20%). The cost impact:

# Cost model: three-tier routing vs. baseline
DAILY_REQUESTS = 500_000

# Tier allocations and per-request costs
tiers = {
    "fast": {"fraction": 0.42, "cost_per_req": 0.0005},   # small model
    "standard": {"fraction": 0.38, "cost_per_req": 0.003},  # mid-tier
    "heavy": {"fraction": 0.20, "cost_per_req": 0.015},    # full model
}

baseline_daily = DAILY_REQUESTS * 0.015

routed_daily = sum(
    DAILY_REQUESTS * t["fraction"] * t["cost_per_req"]
    for t in tiers.values()
)

print(f"Baseline daily cost : ${baseline_daily:,.2f}")
print(f"Routed daily cost   : ${routed_daily:,.2f}")
print(f"Daily saving        : ${baseline_daily - routed_daily:,.2f}")
print(f"Monthly saving      : ${(baseline_daily - routed_daily) * 30:,.2f}")
print(f"Reduction           : {(1 - routed_daily / baseline_daily) * 100:.1f}%")

# Output (approximate):
# Baseline daily cost : $7,500.00
# Routed daily cost   : $1,840.00
# Daily saving        : $5,660.00
# Monthly saving      : $169,800.00
# Reduction           : 75.5%

The team deploys with a 30-day shadow mode, validating that the fast-path accuracy (fraction of FAQ responses rated satisfactory) stays above 93% before enabling live routing. They also implement a per-tenant override that forces sensitive tenants' traffic directly to the heavy tier regardless of complexity score, satisfying contractual quality commitments.
Common mistakes
Routing on request length alone

Prompt length is a useful signal but not sufficient on its own. A 50-token prompt asking to "fix the concurrency bug in the attached code" is trivially short but requires deep reasoning. Length should be one feature among several, not the sole criterion.
No validation after generation
Routing a request to the fast tier and returning whatever the small model produces without checking quality is a silent quality degradation. The response validator closes this loop.

# WRONG: return blindly
async def bad_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    model = route(prompt)
    response = await llm_call(model, prompt)
    return response  # could be empty or malformed

# RIGHT: validate and escalate
async def good_call(prompt: str) -> str:
    tier = route(prompt)
    models_to_try = get_escalation_path(tier)
    for model in models_to_try:
        response = await llm_call(model, prompt)
        if is_valid(response):  # completeness + schema check
            return response
    return response  # last resort
Best practices
Start with two tiers, not four.

The fast/standard split captures most of the savings. Adding specialist and fallback tiers is worthwhile after validating the two-tier system. Each additional tier multiplies the classification surface and the monitoring burden.
Log routing decisions with ground truth.

Every routing decision should be logged with the tier chosen, the model used, the token count, and eventually a quality signal (human rating, downstream success metric, or automated scorer). This data enables classifier retraining and audit.
Make the router synchronous and fast.

The router's classification latency directly adds to the end-to-end request latency. Rule-based classifiers run in microseconds; lightweight embedding classifiers run in 10–30 ms. Avoid using a full LLM call as the classifier in the hot path.
Normalise the API surface across tiers.

Callers should interact with a single unified interface. The router handles model selection, prompt formatting differences, and response normalisation internally. Leaking tier details to callers creates coupling that makes tier changes painful.
Set conservative escalation thresholds initially.

Err toward the heavier model when uncertain. A 5% over-routing rate (sending simple requests to a heavier model) is much less damaging than a 5% under-routing rate (sending complex requests to the fast path). Tighten the threshold as the classifier matures.
Build a canary route.

Reserve 1–5% of traffic for the full model regardless of classification. This provides continuous ground-truth signal on whether the fast and standard paths are actually producing equivalent quality — without it, quality regressions on the light paths can go undetected for days.
Performance & scalability
The routing decision itself must complete well under 50 ms to avoid dominating the response time for fast-path requests. The components that affect routing latency are:
Classification latency:

Rule-based: < 1 ms. Embedding similarity: 10–50 ms (dominated by embedding model call). Meta-model (LLM-as-router): 200–800 ms — viable only if the downstream model call takes seconds, not for fast-path work.
Cache lookup:
A Redis-backed exact-match or vector-similarity cache adds 1–5 ms for a cache hit but can eliminate the model call entirely, returning in total under 10 ms for cached responses.
Health check overhead:

Polling endpoint health metrics on every request is too slow. Health state should be maintained in a background process that pushes updates to a shared in-memory structure (e.g. a Python dict or Redis hash) read by the router on each call.

At very high throughput (tens of thousands of requests per second), the router itself can become a bottleneck. Design it as a stateless function that can be horizontally scaled. Because routing decisions depend only on the request and the health state (which can be replicated), multiple router instances can run in parallel without coordination overhead.

For batch workloads, routing can be done asynchronously: classify all requests in a batch first, then dispatch in parallel to appropriate backends, collecting results. This pipelines classification and generation, hiding the classification latency inside the network round-trip time for the first model calls.

attrs.latex: \text{Effective throughput} = \frac{N_{\text{fast}} \cdot T_{\text{fast}}^{-1} + N_{\text{std}} \cdot T_{\text{std}}^{-1} + N_{\text{heavy}} \cdot T_{\text{heavy}}^{-1}}{N_{\text{total}}}

content.text: Where
is the request count per tier and
is the mean generation time per tier. Increasing
(the fast-path fraction) directly increases effective throughput even without adding any compute.
Tooling & ecosystem
Tool / Platform
Category
Key capability
LiteLLM
Proxy / gateway
Unified OpenAI-compatible API across 100+ providers; built-in fallback and load balancing; per-model spend limits
OpenRouter
API aggregator
Single API key routes to many providers; automatic model fallback; price-sorted routing
RouteLLM
Routing library
Open-source learned router (BERT-based classifier) trained on preference data; configurable quality-cost trade-off threshold
Portkey AI
Observability + gateway
Semantic caching, conditional routing based on metadata, request logging with cost attribution
AWS Bedrock
Managed inference
Intelligent prompt routing feature that automatically selects the most cost-effective model meeting a quality target
Redis / Upstash
Response cache
Low-latency semantic cache storage; supports both exact-match and vector-similarity cache entries
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Cost control
Latency
Quality flexibility
Operational complexity
Request routing (multi-tier)
High (50–80% savings)
Excellent on fast path; standard on others
Per-request adaptation
High — multiple models, classifier maintenance
Single model (large)
Low — all traffic pays premium cost
Consistent but high
Uniform max quality
Low — single endpoint
Single model (small)
High — all traffic on cheap model
Excellent
Uniformly limited
Low
Prompt caching only
Moderate — reduces repeat cost
Good on cache hits
No quality adaptation
Low
Speculative decoding
Indirect — reduces tokens generated
Excellent (2–3x speedup)
Same quality as target model
Moderate — requires draft model
Security & reliability
Adversarial routing manipulation

A malicious caller can craft prompts specifically designed to appear simple to the classifier while actually encoding a complex or harmful request. For example, a prompt that uses terse phrasing or unusual Unicode representations to avoid keyword triggers while embedding a prompt injection payload. The router's fast path should not be considered a security boundary — safety filtering must happen independently of the routing decision, either before routing or on every tier.
Tier degradation and failover

When a backend tier is degraded — elevated latency, high error rates, or a provider outage — the router must detect this within seconds and redirect traffic. Health state should be updated from a background health monitor using exponential moving averages of error rate and latency p95. Circuit breakers should open within 5–10 failed requests and remain open for a configurable cooldown (typically 30–60 seconds) before probing with a small fraction of traffic.

import time
from collections import deque
from threading import Lock

class CircuitBreaker:
    def __init__(self, failure_threshold: int = 5, cooldown_seconds: float = 30.0):
        self.failure_threshold = failure_threshold
        self.cooldown_seconds = cooldown_seconds
        self._failures: deque = deque(maxlen=failure_threshold)
        self._open_until: float = 0.0
        self._lock = Lock()

    @property
    def is_open(self) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            return time.monotonic() < self._open_until

    def record_failure(self) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            self._failures.append(time.monotonic())
            if len(self._failures) == self.failure_threshold:
                self._open_until = time.monotonic() + self.cooldown_seconds

    def record_success(self) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            self._failures.clear()

# In the router dispatch path:
# if breaker.is_open:
#     route to next tier rather than this endpoint
Cost cap enforcement

Without budget guardrails at the router, a single malfunctioning agent that enters a retry loop can exhaust a per-user or per-organisation token quota in minutes. Implement per-caller token budgets as atomic counters in Redis. Before dispatching to any model, atomically check and decrement the budget; if the budget is exhausted, return a structured error rather than proceeding. This prevents cost amplification from runaway agents.
Key takeaways
Request routing dispatches each incoming task to the most cost-appropriate backend before any generation, using features like complexity score, domain tag, SLA class, and current endpoint health.
The primary benefit is cost reduction of 50–80% on mixed-complexity workloads, alongside lower tail latency for simple tasks and higher effective throughput from heavy model endpoints.

Misclassification is asymmetric — routing a complex task to the fast path silently degrades quality, while over-routing a simple task to the heavy path wastes money but produces a correct answer. Calibrate conservatively and validate with a response checker.
Start with a rule-based heuristic classifier (no model inference overhead), validate against ground-truth data, then evolve to an embedding-based or learned classifier as signal accumulates.
In multi-turn agentic sessions, track session tier and only escalate — never downgrade mid-session — to avoid losing conversation context or producing inconsistent quality across turns.
Safety filtering must operate independently of routing decisions; the fast path is a cost optimisation, not a security boundary.
Deploy with shadow mode before cutting over live traffic, and run a continuous canary route (1–5% of traffic to the full model) to detect quality regressions on lighter tiers immediately.
